speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January 9, 1997. We're interviewing survivor Walter Hartmann. The interviewer is Ronald Bliwas. The city is Hammond, Indiana. The st-- excuse me-- the state is Indiana. The country is the United States of America, and the language that it's going to be conducted in is English. I'm Ronald Bliwas, R-O-N-A-L-D B-L-I-W-A-S. Today is January 9, 1997, and we're going to be interviewing Walter Hartmann, W-A-L-T-E-R, Hartmann, H-A-R-T-M-A-N-N. Yes. Could you please tell me your name and spell it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my name is Walter Hartmann, W-A-L-T-E-R, H-A-R-T-M-A- double N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: That it was. That's why I've kept the two N's.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: October 17, 1922.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chemnitz. Chemnitz, which was originally, before World War I, was a kingdom. When I was born, it was a province, and it was a fairly largish industrial city.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell me about your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. I-- of, of them, I only knew my father's father. He had come to Chemnitz early in life, and he was a flower trader. He had a flower wholesale business, I think. He was married to Flora, nee Fuchs. My mother's father lived most of his life and died in Schrimm-- S-C-R-- S-C-H-R-I- double M in the province of Posen. Posna, they'd say to me. He had a men's clothing store and quite a few children, seven-- three boys and four daughters-- and made, apparently, a rather good living. His wife was Helena, born Fr-- Freit-- Freitag, who died, I'm afraid, fairly young, I think, of cancer, I think. Those are the grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: The only grandfather that you really knew was your mother's-- can you-- can you tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father's father. Can you please tell me about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was a really marvelous little man. I do remember him. Of course, I was of young age. He used to live with my father's sisters in Chemnitz, and he visited fairly often. And I do remember he always wore a-- he always wore a cap, driver's cap. And they had bent chairs, bent chairs. So when I came, he always had to get out of his armchair, give me his cap, so that I could sit in the armchair, take one of the chairs, and play driver. And he always did. I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when he-- when he died? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Must have been eight or nine or something like that. Earlier, perhaps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a religious man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was a learned man. And he was a-- I don't know that he kept all the Orthodox usages, but he was a highly learned man, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go to the synagogue with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I think by the time I knew him, he was a bit old to move very much from the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your father and mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. My mother was one of the-- clan out of Schrimm. Her eldest brother had started a factory-- I forget exactly what-- in a place quite fairly near to Chemnitz. And she came and, and did some work there. My suspicion is-- I don't know this-- but my suspicion is also to find a husband. My father, who was a little older than she, was studying law. And as I understand it, he took one look at her, and then wouldn't leave her alone. And however, he went to war, the First World War, of course. And they married after he came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hans. Hans Hartmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Regina.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Bor-- born name, of course, Flieg. Became Hartmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the circumstances under which they met?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. I don't-- precisely, I don't know, except that Hans was studying law, studying to be a lawyer. And I suppose hung around with various groups, and so did Regina, and my guess is that they met fairly casually. And my understanding was that he, he was very impressed with right from the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your father's education.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he went to what was generally called the humanistic gymnasium. In Germany you had-- and I suppose you now have, I don't know-- four years of elementary school, and then you had a decision. You could go continue elementary school, or you could go what in Germany was called high school. And any halfway sizable city had a number of them, and they ranged all the way from very practical and scientific, over to what we might call classical, or as I said, humanistic, where they went very strongly on Latin and Greek and humanities and so on. And my father went to one of these gymnasia. And then, he went to university and studied law.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about his religious education?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very interesting. The way we lived for most of the time was pretty much as people live here, with a very attenuated connection to actual religion. But when we got a little more interested and a few more things were done, it occurred to me-- and now, looking back on it, I'm fairly certain that he was actually quite well-educated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he bar mitzvahed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Undoubtedly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Undoubtedly. How about your mother's education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, that's a little strange. I know she went to something that we would here consider high school. I don't know that she went to anything further, but she was a very well-read woman, and could and did give anybody a good conversation, a good discussion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have a younger brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, his name in Germany was Horst, but since nobody can say this in English-- they always say horse or hearse-- he adopted Henry, as a name when he became a naturalized American citizen. So he's now Henry Horst Hartmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years difference is there between you?

speaker: interviewee
text: About three and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three and a half years. Tell me about your home. The--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. When I was born, we lived reasonably modestly, I think, in a not so very large, but very nice apartment. I gather, now, looking back on it-- we didn't discuss these things. We weren't told about this. But I guess my father was beginning to make a name for himself as a lawyer. And in-- that was-- I was born '22. And then, I think it was in 1929-- give or take a little-- that we then moved to-- something I've never quite understood. It was a large apartment, and my suspicion is that it was something like what we would call a condominium. I think-- I remember we owned it because my mother was able to put all kinds of things in and change all kinds of things around, or she had some other arrangement with whoever owned it. That was-- that was the apartment that, you know, that I remember most. That was my [INAUDIBLE] home in Germany-- quite luxurious and quite very pleasant.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the address?

speaker: interviewee
text: That address was 26 [GERMAN] Strasse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it primarily a Jewish area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, it was not. Indeed, as I recall it, there wasn't a specifically Jewish area in Chemnitz. There were a fair amount of Jews living there. The, the synagogue was a considerable one. But I don't think there was a Jewish area.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Talk about your father for a second. You'd mentioned earlier that he was in the First World War.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was. As I understand it, he didn't have to go. He was on his way to make various steps to become a lawyer, and he didn't have to go, but he insisted. He became a you might say-- well, I think he joined the army in 1915 or '16. He joined the infantry, was wounded. I have somewhere the document that you give him, what was the equivalent, in those days, of the German Purple Heart. He was given the Iron Cross, Second Class. He was put in for First Class, but they didn't give it to him. And he had a Saxon Silver Medal, a Royal Medal. He was wounded toward the end of the war, '17, I think, or '18, and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, did he ever talk to you about the First World War?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very little. Obviously as a kid, I was very interested in my father's heroic aspects, but to get him to talk about the war was practically impossible. Look, he was a-- he was a infantry soldier in the front lines, both in Russia and in France. I can't understand why he wouldn't talk about it. The only story I ever heard about it was that when he got home on leave, and would I guess what he was most craving coming home on leave. And I couldn't guess, and the answer was a glass of water-- fresh, real water. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Even so, I have a letter somewhere in which he wrote to my mother-- they were more or less engaged, I suppose, at that time-- to my mother that he hoped that Germany wouldn't give up, that he was quite ready to go again if necessary, even though what he was getting-- going into was obviously not very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for me, first of all, the, the-- what languages did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just German. In your home, can you describe for me, were there a lot of religious articles and literature?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much literature, as I recall. There were articles. We had a beautiful silver besamim box, or the, the spice box, and Shabbat candle holders and various things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- w-- would you consider your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not for a long while. Toward the end-- '37 to '38, somewhere around there-- all of a sudden, my mother made Kiddush Friday evenings. I was able to make contact with the local rabbi, and asked him an awful lot of questions. And he was very sweet, and, and, you know, discussed things with me for hours. So I suspect you might say we became more obviously religious as time went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before '37 or '38, can you remember what rituals-- religious rituals-- you observed in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was always seder, of course. Hanukkah. Naturally, the high holidays, when you went to Synagogue. In Germany, I, I often got to carry my father's hat box, because at that time in Germany, the gentlemen were likely to wear silk hats in the synagogue, at least the evening before.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you had a seder, did you have relatives there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, usually lots of people, lost of people. My mother's cooking was quite attractive, apparently.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, do you remember anything at all about the seders?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember one or two things. I always was a little annoyed that people didn't take it more seriously. They'd make jokes and stuff like that. It-- I, I think it was relatively typical. I've been to many sidarim since, of course, and I don't they are materially different from what we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, did you have a big family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, fairly big. Now, many of them, of course had their own sidarim. But a number of them would show up, yes. And friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a lot of cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Hanukkah. Do you remember Hanukkah?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was more private. I mean, not many people came in. But we had-- the Hanukkah candles would be lit, and we would get seven or eight presents.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll tell you something, which might be of some interest, about my grandfather, who, as I told you, was very religious indeed and very learned. And he asked my mother did we have a Christmas tree? And she looked at him quite surprised and said, coming from you, of course not. Said, no, you should. You should. It's a very nice habit. It's not religious. It's a memory of a-- of a midwinter light festival. So he was, in a sense, I suppose, you might say very liberal. But I don't recall Christmas trees in our home. So now, there was only one synagogue in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your religious education.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the, the general thing was that in Germany, the region was more or less supported-- somewhat supported anyway-- by the government. For example, we could get religious education by being freed from the school. That was-- that was set up that way. As I recall it, though, la-- a little later, I suppose you might say bar mitzvah preparation, I'm not sure. But we would meet more or less in private, a small group of us-- more or less in private-- with a cantor of the congregation. And he was trying to knock some Hebrew into us without much success, I'm afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was, was it a big Jewish congregation?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I thought about this. It always seemed big to me. But, you see, Chemnitz had something in the neighborhood-- I think I'm right on this, but don't-- if you want to look it up in the Judaica-- I think it had somewhere like maybe 200,000 people. And I would think that the total number of Jews probably was not much less than 2,000, I would think. But that's-- I'm guessing, now. I don't know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe, in the-- in your youth growing up really before Hitler came into power, the relationship between the Jews and the non-Jews. Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know too much about that. I don't know we thought much about it. We didn't talk much about it. I know my father was very popular, I mean, as a lawyer, and had a great many clients. I don't know that my mother engaged with a great many non-Jewish women, but she might have. I'm sorry. I really--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not clear on that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have a lot of non-Jewish clients?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Quite a few, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: He did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And some very big ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- what was his specialty? Did he have a--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's very interesting. As far as I can make out now, later on as I got a little bit older, I would go into his office from school, and pretend I was being useful. What I was really doing was looking at files which I shouldn't have looked at. And I got the impression he was really quite all around. He certainly was what we would call, I suppose, a commercial lawyer. There, there were quite a few-- I get-- there was a commercial court that he was admitted to. He had a-- he took family. I know that he did a few divorces. And I also know that occasionally he went into a criminal case. I know that because when he did, he would bring home his gown, and very elegant, and put it on and show us. And then he would wear it in court. So I would say he was a general lawyer with some emphasis on commercial and industrial work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did your family or members of your family belong to a lot of Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. Depends on what you mean by a lot. My father, of course, was prominent in the congregation. He was on the-- on the-- on the board an so on. He was fairly active in B'nai B'rith. Indeed, he was president of the B'nai B'rith in Chemnitz during the Jubilee Year. He was interested in, and finally took, in the very end-- he took the leadership in a sports club [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What else? He became on the board and then director of the Jewish Bank that existed, Ivria Bank. That's all I can think of right now. Let's talk a little bit more about, about your family. Did you take family vacations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I remember, I suppose there was a rather pleasant resort not very far from Chemnitz called Augustusburg, and we went a few times there, enjoying ourselves quite a lot. We also lived near the Erzgebirge, that is the Ore Mountains, and that was the boundary with Czechoslovakia. And we went into Czechoslovakia a few times, enjoying ourselves hugely. We stayed in what they call a penzion, which is not a hotel. It was a private house that I suppose we'd say board and breakfast, I suppose. But then, three summers, and I recall. One time we went to Gargellen, in Vorarlberg. These are the Alps in Austria. A second time, we went to Pontresina in the Bernina Alps in Switzerland. That's fairly close to St. Moritz, which we visited. And a third time-- I think that was already in Nazi times, I believe-- we went to Siusi in the Dolomites in Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And primarily on these trips it was your mother and father and you and brother? Yes. In Siusi I happen to remember that there was another family from Chemnitz that we kind of joined up with a little bit, but generally speaking, it was, yes, he, she, and us. When, when you-- you mentioned traveling during, during the time of the Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a difference in the way you were-- you traveled? Did you have to get extra passports? What-- tell me about that, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Actually, no. We, we did the same things, but they were under kind of a different sign. For example, fairly soon after Hitler came to power, the passports of my parents were stamped J, for Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: A little later, the Nazis called out a list of Jewish names, which we are allowed to have. And if you didn't have one, you, if you were a male, you were-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was ordered that you take the name Israel. If you were a female and didn't have one of the accepted Jewish names, you were supposed to add Sarah. So the passports that I have, the last passports of my parents, are for Hans Israel Hartmann and Regina Sarah Hartmann, you see? And you had that on your passports too?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have passports. I, I traveled on my parents'.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Do you remember how you felt? Did your parents talk about having to add the names to their passports? Was there any discussion about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there are two things. Number one, my brother and I were obviously spoiled children. I mean we had, you know-- we had a great deal going for us, see? And then my parents-- in my opinion wrongly-- but my parents were determined that we should be protected from everything as far as possible, as much as possible. So no, there wasn't much discussion.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a young person before Hitler came into power, do you remember any of the goals or aspirations that you had as a child? Do you remember thinking, I wanted to grow up to be whatever?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, nothing really firm or really definite. It was more or less accepted-- it was understood-- that my brother and I would go to high school and probably this humanistic gymnasium. And then we'd probably go to university somewhere. I myself, as a kid, wasn't aware-- averse to becoming a lawyer. I mean, you know, I saw my father doing very nicely, thank you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But you see-- OK. Hitler was in power. You see, I was-- it was 1933 that I went to high school as Hitler had just come to power. And I asked my father. I said, look, wouldn't-- don't you think that with things being as they are, perhaps I should go into a school a little more practical a little more definite, you know, specific, since we may wish to emigrate, than this humanistic stuff. And I remember very well. My father said, A, he was very pleased that I thought along those lines. B, he thought that what I said made sense. However, his own experience was that the humanistic education was the best foundation for pretty much anything you want to be, whether you're going to be a shoemaker or a lawyer or a plumber or an industrial worker. He felt that the humanistic background was the most efficient. And I myself found it was so, for at least for a guy like me. I don't know if it'd be true for everybody, but for me, it worked out very nicely, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, when you were young, what were your hobbies? What did you enjoy doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I played with my brother quite a bit, you know, imaginative games. And I read quite a lot. And there was some schoolwork, some homework. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that was about as far as it g-- went, as far as I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. By and large, I did. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Interestingly enough, even under the Nazis-- you see, my elementary school period ended in '33. I was 11 and went to this humanistic gymnasium. The point was that this was known as a staatsgymnasium, the state gymnasium, which meant that it was under the direct control of the state of Saxony, you see, province. All the other schools, high schools, were under the control of the municipality of Chemnitz. This gave the faculty and the director of the school some leeway. Now for example, we stayed in the-- the few Jews who were there were kept in this staatsgymnasium longer than any other Jew. And the faculty, as far as they could, did, somehow protect ourselves. I recall a little story which is, I suppose, half funny and half serious. I came in one day, and on my desk were all kinds of remarks chalked in, in chalk. And also the the newspaper was there. was a newspaper which was [GERMAN], and it was a pornographic thing. It was a, you know-- telling you how awful the Jews were and so on, so on. It was on many street corners. They had their reading box, and I always stayed and looked at it and read it. And I think I was the only one who did.

speaker: interviewer
text: However, this young man had to put this on my desk. And I said, who did this? And he stuck his face into mine and said, I did, you know? So I thought myself, look, might just as well be knocked about one way as the other. So I fetched out and gave him the most satisfying smack in the face I have ever done.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it seemed like nothing happened. I expected the classroom to go after me, but it didn't. Indeed, the particular young man came and asked me back for the and when my back was turned, he cleaned up the desk and so on and so on. They were afraid, especially of the teachers, who they thought would support me.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's a sequence to this story, which I learned much later. I happened to be out with my mother to visit some of her friends, and mother then was talking to me, oh yes, this is so and so, the heroic Walter. You know, how am I heroic Walter, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I got it out of my mother. She didn't want to tell us this. But the boy whom I had hit, his father was a Stormtroop officer, a Stormtroop bigshot. And he called my father and he said he didn't appreciate his son being attacked by, you know, by this Jewish pig or whatever. And my father apparently told him very quietly that he never interferes in his son's school problems, but he was pleased to hear that he was able to stand up for himself, and hung up.
